choices.outcome: The economic response addressed the underlying grievance rather than just the symptom. Violence wound down. Community leaders called it the first time federal action addressed the actual cause.

title: US Casualties Report Triggers Congressional Backlash
description: The Pentagon has released updated casualty figures from the active conflict. 47 American service members killed in the past 30 days. Gold Star families are testifying before Congress. The antiwar movement has found new momentum and three senators from your own party are calling for an exit strategy.

choices.text: Hold a prime-time address honoring the fallen and reaffirming the mission
choices.outcome: The address was dignified and moved the country. The antiwar coalition lost some momentum. Your base consolidated behind the mission for another month.

choices.text: Announce a phased drawdown — begin reducing troop levels
choices.outcome: The drawdown announcement was enormously popular domestically. Allies expressed concern. The conflict's trajectory became less certain without the full commitment.

choices.text: Escalate — request emergency war funding from Congress to end it faster
choices.outcome: The escalation request triggered the largest antiwar protests since Vietnam. Three senators announced they would vote to defund the operation. The conflict intensified.

choices.text: Accelerate peace negotiations — appoint a special envoy
choices.outcome: The envoy appointment signaled seriousness about ending the conflict. Talks began within two weeks. Progress was slow but the political pressure eased significantly.

title: Half a Million March on Washington Against the War
description: The largest antiwar demonstration in two decades has descended on the National Mall. Polls show 58% of Americans now oppose continued military involvement. Your Defense Secretary is urging you to hold firm. Your Chief of Staff is warning that the war is becoming your defining liability.

choices.text: Meet with protest leaders — acknowledge their concerns publicly
choices.outcome: The gesture was widely praised as presidential. Your base was frustrated by the optics of meeting with antiwar groups. The protest movement felt heard and partially de-escalated.

choices.text: Stay the course — publicly dismiss the protests as uninformed
choices.outcome: The dismissal galvanized the movement. The following weekend saw protests in 60 cities. Your approval with independents dropped 8 points in a week.

choices.text: Announce a clear exit timeline — war ends within 18 months
choices.outcome: The timeline announcement drained the protest movement of its urgency. Allies were alarmed. The adversary immediately began waiting you out. The country exhaled.

choices.text: Request a joint congressional resolution of support for the mission
choices.outcome: The resolution failed to pass. The vote revealed exactly how thin congressional support had become. The defeat emboldened the antiwar caucus and became the story.

title: Adversary Signals Willingness to Negotiate Ceasefire
description: Through backchannels, the opposing side in the active conflict has indicated a willingness to discuss terms. Your military advisors say the battlefield position is favorable — pushing harder now could produce a better outcome. Your diplomatic team says this window may close within 30 days.

choices.text: Accept the opening — begin formal negotiations immediately
choices.outcome: Negotiations began within the week. Progress was real if imperfect. A ceasefire framework was announced two months later to enormous international relief.

choices.text: Reject it — negotiate from a position of complete victory instead
choices.outcome: The rejection kept the conflict going. The battlefield position improved marginally over the next three months. The window for negotiation closed. The war continued.

choices.text: Engage secretly while maintaining public military posture
choices.outcome: The secret talks produced a framework. The leak came three weeks later. The optics of secret diplomacy were mixed — some called it sophisticated, others called it deceptive.

choices.text: Demand preconditions before talks — public prisoner exchange first
choices.outcome: The preconditions were accepted. The prisoner exchange was emotional and politically powerful. Formal negotiations followed a month later with better public support.

title: Congress Moves to Defund the Military Operation
description: A bipartisan coalition of 218 House members has introduced legislation to cut off funding for the ongoing military operation within 60 days. The vote is next week. If it passes, you face a constitutional standoff. Your JAG advisors are split on whether you can continue operations without congressional authorization.

choices.text: Work with Congress — negotiate a structured wind-down with funding
choices.outcome: The negotiated wind-down gave the military 90 days to draw down responsibly. Allies were notified. The withdrawal was more orderly than most feared.

choices.text: Veto the bill and invoke emergency executive war powers
choices.outcome: The veto triggered a constitutional crisis. The Supreme Court agreed to hear an expedited case on executive war powers. The standoff consumed everything for two months.

choices.text: Accept the defunding and begin immediate withdrawal
choices.outcome: The withdrawal was rapid and chaotic. Critics called the images devastating. The troops came home. 58% of Americans approved of the decision in the moment.

choices.text: Ask for 6 more months and a diplomatic milestone — negotiate with the bill's authors
choices.outcome: The compromise bought six months and a specific diplomatic benchmark. The timeline created pressure that actually accelerated negotiations. A workable exit.

title: Key Ally Announces Withdrawal from the Coalition
description: Your most significant military coalition partner has announced it will withdraw its forces from the joint operation within 30 days, citing domestic political pressure. The decision leaves a significant gap in air support and intelligence sharing. Other allies are watching to see how you respond.

choices.text: Publicly pressure the ally to reverse course
choices.outcome: The public pressure backfired. The ally doubled down and two more coalition partners began signaling they might follow. The alliance frayed visibly.

choices.text: Accept the withdrawal privately — fill the gap with US assets
choices.outcome: The quiet acceptance avoided a public rupture. The additional US commitment cost more than expected. The coalition's public appearance of unity was maintained.

choices.text: Use it as leverage — offer trade concessions to keep them in
choices.outcome: The concessions worked. The ally stayed. The trade deal drew criticism at home as paying for allies to do what they committed to. Worth it, strategically.

choices.text: Treat it as the signal to reassess the whole operation
choices.outcome: The reassessment produced a modified mission with a clearer endpoint. The ally's withdrawal became the catalyst for a smarter strategy. Critics called it retreat. History may disagree.

title: Major Military Objective Achieved — Define the Endgame
description: US forces have achieved the primary military objective that justified the operation. The adversary's command structure is degraded. The immediate threat is neutralized. Now comes the hardest question in American foreign policy: what does victory actually look like, and when do we leave?

choices.text: Declare victory and begin immediate responsible withdrawal
choices.outcome: The declaration was celebrated domestically. The withdrawal proceeded over 90 days. The long-term stability of the region remained uncertain but the immediate mission was complete.

choices.text: Stay to rebuild — commit to a nation-building phase
choices.outcome: The nation-building mission expanded the commitment significantly. Progress was real but slow. The American public's patience began running out within six months.

choices.text: Transfer security responsibility to local forces and draw down
choices.outcome: The transfer approach was the most durable option. Local forces held. The draw-down took four months. A genuine success story with room for critics to argue about timing.

choices.text: Expand the mission — address secondary threats while positioned
choices.outcome: The mission expansion was widely condemned as mission creep. The additional objectives proved harder than anticipated. The war's cost in blood and treasure continued climbing.

title: Second Wave Hits — Hospitals Overwhelmed in 8 States
description: A more transmissible variant has triggered a second pandemic wave. Hospital ICU capacity is at 94% in eight states. The death rate is climbing. The public is exhausted and compliance with public health guidance has dropped dramatically. Your previous decisions have shaped what tools you have available now.

choices.text: Coordinate federal field hospital deployment and medical supply surge
choices.outcome: The federal response reduced mortality in the hardest-hit states by an estimated 22%. The cost was significant but the competence was undeniable. A genuine public health win.

choices.text: Implement targeted regional restrictions in overwhelmed areas
choices.outcome: The restrictions were deeply unpopular and compliance was inconsistent. The hospital pressure eased in six weeks. The economic and political cost was significant.

choices.text: Accelerate booster rollout and launch a national vaccination push
choices.outcome: The vaccination campaign reduced severe illness rates within eight weeks. The anti-vaccine segment was unmoved. The overall public health trajectory improved meaningfully.

choices.text: Declare the pandemic over — let individuals and states manage risk
choices.outcome: The declaration was premature by any epidemiological measure. Deaths continued climbing for six more weeks. The political and human cost of the 'pandemic is over' framing was severe.

title: Vaccine Misinformation Driving Dangerous Hesitancy
description: Coordinated misinformation campaigns — some traced to foreign actors — have pushed vaccine hesitancy to 34% nationally. Outbreaks are concentrated in unvaccinated communities. Hospital systems in these regions are near collapse. The line between public health and political identity has never been sharper.

choices.text: Launch a trusted-messenger campaign with community leaders and doctors
choices.outcome: The local-voice approach moved hesitancy rates more than federal messaging alone. Vaccination rates improved 8 points over two months in the targeted communities.

choices.text: Mandate vaccines for federal employees and contractors
choices.outcome: The mandate triggered massive political backlash. Legal challenges flooded in. Vaccination rates among the mandated population hit 94%. The political cost was severe.

choices.text: Sanction the social media platforms spreading misinformation
choices.outcome: The platforms modified their policies under pressure. Misinformation spread shifted to alternative platforms. A meaningful partial victory in a battle that had no clean win.

choices.text: Offer financial incentives — $200 payments for vaccination proof
choices.outcome: The incentive program moved 4 million previously hesitant Americans to get vaccinated. Public health experts praised the pragmatism. Fiscal hawks criticized the cost.

title: Pandemic Economic Damage Deeper Than Projected
description: The economic data is in. The pandemic has cost the economy an estimated $3.8 trillion in lost output. Small businesses in hospitality, retail, and entertainment are closing permanently. Unemployment in affected sectors is at 12%. The recovery is real but uneven — and the political pain is acute.

choices.text: Launch a targeted small business recovery and retraining fund
choices.outcome: The recovery fund reached 2.1 million small businesses. Closures slowed. The program became one of the more visible economic competence wins of the term.

choices.text: Broad stimulus checks — $1,400 to every American adult
choices.outcome: Consumer spending rebounded fast. Inflation ticked up in ways that took months to fully register with the public. The checks were enormously popular in the short term.

choices.text: Focus on deficit reduction — position for long-term fiscal health
choices.outcome: The austerity position was economically defensible and politically catastrophic. Working class voters who expected help felt abandoned. The recovery took longer than necessary.

choices.text: Invest in pandemic-proof infrastructure — remote work, broadband, healthcare capacity
choices.outcome: The forward-looking investment built genuine long-term resilience. The benefits took 18 months to become visible. Voters wanted help now. The policy was right but the timing was hard.

title: WHO Declares Pandemic Over — Navigate the Aftermath
description: The World Health Organization has officially declared the end of the public health emergency. The immediate crisis is over but the aftermath is everywhere — 180,000 Americans died on your watch, the economy is still healing, and the political reckoning over how it was handled is just beginning.

choices.text: Hold a national day of mourning and take responsibility for what went wrong
choices.outcome: The accountability and grief were genuine and widely appreciated. Approval climbed 7 points in a week. Your base wanted celebration, not mourning. The country needed both.

choices.text: Declare victory — emphasize lives saved and economic resilience
choices.outcome: The victory framing landed with your base and alienated grieving families. Critics found the tone-deafness politically damaging. The country was more divided on the legacy than the announcement assumed.

choices.text: Announce a pandemic preparedness commission and permanent response infrastructure
choices.outcome: The preparedness investment was praised by public health experts globally. The commission produced a landmark report. Future administrations would benefit from the institutional foundation you built.

choices.text: Move on — shift focus entirely to economic recovery
choices.outcome: The pivot to economics played well with business groups. Families who lost people felt the transition was too abrupt. A pragmatic choice with a real human cost in how it felt.

title: Third Quarter GDP Confirms Deepening Recession
description: The third consecutive quarter of negative growth confirms the recession is worsening, not stabilizing. Unemployment has now hit 7.8%. Consumer confidence is at a 15-year low. Your economic team's previous projections have been wrong twice. The public has stopped believing the optimistic forecasts.

choices.text: Appoint a new economic team — visible change in approach
choices.outcome: The team change signaled responsiveness without admitting full failure. The new Treasury advisor's first press conference moved markets up 2%. A credible reset.

choices.text: Double down on the current strategy — the data will turn
choices.outcome: The data did not turn for two more quarters. The public stopped listening to economic projections from your team entirely. The credibility gap became the story.

choices.text: Emergency job guarantee program — federal employer of last resort
choices.outcome: The program employed 1.2 million people within six months. The debate about government's role in the economy dominated the rest of the term. Unemployment fell measurably.

choices.text: Request emergency Fed coordination — joint statement on recovery path
choices.outcome: The coordinated statement stabilized market expectations. The Fed's credibility amplified the effect of your economic messaging. A genuine technical win.

title: First Signs of Recovery — Manage the Narrative
description: For the first time in three quarters, GDP growth turned positive at 1.2%. Unemployment ticked down 0.3%. The recovery is fragile and early, but real. How you handle this moment — underselling or overselling it — will define how voters remember the recession.

choices.text: Announce cautious optimism — 'we're turning the corner, not there yet'
choices.outcome: The calibrated tone was credible. Voters appreciated the honesty. When the recovery continued, you got full credit for not overclaiming. A rare case where restraint paid off.

choices.text: Declare the recession over and take credit for the recovery
choices.outcome: The declaration was premature — another 0.2% growth quarter followed. The overclaim became the story when economists pushed back. The partial win turned into a credibility question.

choices.text: Focus on workers who haven't felt the recovery yet — announce more aid
choices.outcome: The focus on left-behind workers was both genuine and politically smart. The additional aid accelerated the recovery's reach. The story became 'recovery reaches everyone, not just Wall Street.'

choices.text: Use the positive data to push a long-term economic reform package
choices.outcome: The recovery window created political space for structural reform. The package passed in modified form. A durable economic foundation built on a moment of relative political calm.

title: Supreme Court Confirmation Becomes a Political War
description: Your Supreme Court nominee's confirmation hearings have turned into a brutal political battle. New background information has surfaced. The opposition is demanding delays. Three senators from your own party are wavering. The nomination that was supposed to be your legacy moment is now threatening to define your term negatively.

choices.text: Stand firm — full-court press to confirm your nominee
choices.outcome: The nominee was confirmed by two votes after six weeks of brutal combat. The process damaged every senator who voted yes. The justice took their seat. Your base celebrated.

choices.text: Withdraw the nomination and choose a less controversial candidate
choices.outcome: The withdrawal looked like a defeat in the moment. The replacement nominee sailed through in two weeks. The seat was filled. Your base never fully forgave you for backing down.

choices.text: Negotiate — offer the wavering senators specific policy concessions
choices.outcome: The deals held. The nominee was confirmed by four votes. The concessions were criticized as transactional. The justice was seated. A win that felt like a negotiation.

choices.text: Delay the vote — buy time for the controversy to fade
choices.outcome: The delay extended the controversy rather than defusing it. Three more damaging stories emerged during the pause. The vote eventually proceeded under worse conditions.

title: Historic Nominee Faces Unexpected Opposition
description: Your historic Supreme Court nominee — the first of their kind — is facing a coordinated opposition campaign questioning their judicial philosophy. The confirmation battle has taken on cultural weight far beyond the usual politics. Every statement you make about it becomes a national news story.

choices.text: Make the historic significance explicit — campaign for your nominee
choices.outcome: The visibility campaign created enormous momentum. The confirmation hearings drew record viewership. The nominee was confirmed 53-47 in a vote that felt historic to millions of Americans.

choices.text: Let the nominee's record speak — stay out of the public debate
choices.outcome: The low-key approach reduced the temperature. The nominee was confirmed in a process that, by Supreme Court standards, was almost orderly. The historic nature was noted without being weaponized.

choices.text: Directly confront the opposition's arguments in a national address
choices.outcome: The address was electrifying for supporters and enraging for opponents. The confirmation happened. The cultural battle it kicked off lasted the rest of the term.

choices.text: Negotiate modifications to the nominee's stated positions
choices.outcome: The attempt to moderate the nominee's positions was seen as capitulation by supporters and insufficient by opponents. The nominee withdrew rather than be reshaped. A significant political loss.

title: Coup in Major African Nation Threatens US Interests
description: A military coup has overthrown the democratically elected government in a nation where the US has significant security partnerships and rare earth mineral agreements. The new junta is already signaling a turn toward China and Russia. 800 US military advisors are in country. The African Union is calling for intervention.

choices.text: Immediately suspend military aid and recognize only the deposed government
choices.outcome: The principled stance was praised internationally. The junta accelerated its pivot to China. US mineral access was suspended within three months. The democracy signal was clear and costly.

choices.text: Maintain relations with the junta — prioritize US security partnerships
choices.outcome: The pragmatic choice preserved access and partnerships but drew sharp condemnation. Human rights organizations launched a major campaign. The junta grew more authoritarian with US acquiescence.

choices.text: Lead an African Union-backed pressure campaign for democratic restoration
choices.outcome: The multilateral approach produced real pressure. Sanctions and diplomatic isolation led to a negotiated transition timeline within four months. A genuine diplomatic success.

choices.text: Evacuate US personnel and disengage entirely
choices.outcome: The disengagement created a vacuum that China filled within weeks. US influence in the region declined significantly. The rare earth agreements were voided. A strategic retreat with lasting consequences.

title: Mass Migration Crisis from Latin America Reaches Breaking Point
description: A combination of gang violence, drought, and economic collapse across Central America has produced the largest migration wave in the hemisphere's history. 400,000 people are moving toward the US border. The situation requires both humanitarian response and diplomatic engagement with six governments simultaneously.

choices.text: Host an emergency regional summit and commit $5B in development aid
choices.outcome: The summit produced the first meaningful regional framework for the migration crisis. The development aid addressed root causes. The flow slowed meaningfully over six months.

choices.text: Seal the border — humanitarian aid at origin only, no new entries
choices.outcome: The closure produced humanitarian disasters at the border that went viral globally. Crossing attempts became more dangerous. The root causes continued unaddressed.

choices.text: Create a temporary protected status program — managed humanitarian entry
choices.outcome: The managed entry program handled 180,000 people in an orderly way. The political opposition was fierce. The humanitarian outcome was meaningfully better than the alternatives.

choices.text: Pressure Mexico to act as a buffer — offer trade incentives for compliance
choices.outcome: Mexico accepted the deal under significant economic pressure. Migration flows to the US border dropped 40%. Mexico's relationship with Central American neighbors deteriorated. A transactional success.

title: Historic Arab-Israeli Normalization Deal Within Reach
description: Your diplomats have quietly brokered a framework for normalization between Israel and a major Arab state that has never had formal relations. The deal would transform the region's geopolitics. It requires US security guarantees that Congress may resist and concessions from both sides that their domestic audiences will hate.

choices.text: Commit fully — provide the security guarantees and push both sides to sign
choices.outcome: The signing ceremony at the White House was the most-watched diplomatic event in a decade. The deal held. Your foreign policy legacy was secured in a single afternoon.

choices.text: Support the deal but let the parties take the credit publicly
choices.outcome: The low-profile approach made the deal more durable. Both parties claimed ownership. US facilitation was documented in every history book. Quiet success is still success.

choices.text: Demand better Palestinian terms before US support — slow it down
choices.outcome: The additional demands were rejected by both parties. The diplomatic window closed. The framework collapsed. Historians would note the US had the deal in hand and declined.

choices.text: Use the leverage to extract trade concessions before signing
choices.outcome: Treating a historic peace deal as a trade negotiation appalled both parties and their international supporters. The deal collapsed. The transactional approach was widely condemned.

title: G7 Summit — Can You Unite the Alliance?
description: The annual G7 summit arrives at a moment of maximum transatlantic tension. Trade disputes, burden-sharing arguments, and competing visions for confronting China have fractured the alliance. As the host, you have one chance to set the agenda and restore coherence. Every major ally is watching.

choices.text: Push a bold joint agenda — China economic competition and climate
choices.outcome: The joint communiqué was the most substantive in a decade. Allied leaders left with a sense that American leadership was back. The follow-through would determine if the summit mattered.

choices.text: Focus on resolving the bilateral trade disputes first
choices.outcome: The trade resolution was practical and meaningful. The larger strategic questions were deferred. Allies appreciated the concrete progress even if they wanted more ambition.

choices.text: Use the summit to announce US withdrawal from several multilateral commitments
choices.outcome: The withdrawal announcements dominated the summit. Allied leaders were furious. The US was isolated within its own alliance. The summit became a symbol of American unilateralism.

choices.text: Build a specific anti-China economic coalition with concrete commitments
choices.outcome: The China-focused coalition was the summit's defining achievement. Six nations committed to supply chain diversification. China issued sharp diplomatic protests. The strategic shift was real.

title: UN Security Council — Your Resolution Is Being Vetoed
description: A US-drafted Security Council resolution on a critical international crisis is being blocked by Russian and Chinese vetoes. The world is watching whether the US can lead through international institutions or is forced to act unilaterally. Your UN Ambassador is asking for guidance.

choices.text: Build a 'coalition of the willing' outside the UN framework
choices.outcome: The coalition assembled 34 nations. The action proceeded without UN authorization. Critics called it a repeat of Iraq-style unilateralism. The security outcome was better than inaction.

choices.text: Accept the veto — work within General Assembly instead
choices.outcome: The General Assembly resolution passed 142-3. It was non-binding but created significant international legitimacy. The actual crisis remained partially unresolved.

choices.text: Negotiate directly with Russia and China to modify the resolution
choices.outcome: The modified resolution passed with abstentions rather than vetoes. The weakened text was less effective but the multilateral legitimacy mattered. A diplomatic compromise.

choices.text: Call out Russia and China publicly — turn it into a transparency moment
choices.outcome: The public call-out was effective messaging that isolated the vetoing powers. The crisis remained unresolved through the UN, but America's principled position was clear to the world.

title: October Surprise — Damaging Story Drops 3 Weeks Before Election
description: Three weeks before the election, a major news organization has published an investigative report containing damaging information. Your communications team has 24 hours to craft a response before it dominates the final stretch of the campaign. How you handle the next 72 hours will determine whether you win.

choices.text: Get ahead of it — full statement, take questions, own the narrative
choices.outcome: The transparency play worked. The story had a 4-day lifespan instead of 3 weeks. Voters respected the directness. You entered election day with the narrative under control.

choices.text: Attack the source — question the journalism and the timing
choices.outcome: The attack energized your base and disgusted everyone else. The story grew. Three more journalists began digging. You spent the final weeks playing defense.

choices.text: Ignore it completely — focus all messaging on your closing argument
choices.outcome: The silence was filled by your opponents. The story stayed alive. Your closing argument got half the airtime you needed. A strategic miscalculation.

choices.text: Counter-program — release positive news to dominate the cycle
choices.outcome: The counter-programming partially worked. The positive announcement split the news cycle. The damaging story remained alive but wasn't the only story. A managed damage limitation.

title: Final Presidential Debate — Defining Moment
description: The final presidential debate is tonight. Your opponent has been gaining in the polls and needs a strong performance. Your debate prep team says you have one clear attack line available that is devastating but risks looking desperate. The stakes could not be higher — 40 million people will watch.

choices.text: Run on your record — strong, specific, presidential
choices.outcome: The performance was disciplined and effective. Independent voters rated you the winner. Your base wanted more fight but the moderates moved your direction. A clean night.

choices.text: Go aggressive — use the opposition research, land the kill shot
choices.outcome: The attack landed but the optics divided the room. Your base roared. Undecided voters called it 'too negative' in the snap polls. A tactical win with strategic risk.

choices.text: Pivot to the future — vision speech, not a defense of the past
choices.outcome: The forward-looking performance connected with undecided voters who were tired of the last four years. Your optimism was genuine and it showed. A strong finish.

choices.text: Challenge your opponent to a specific policy commitment on live TV
choices.outcome: The live challenge was a high-risk moment that paid off. Your opponent stumbled trying to answer. The clip dominated post-debate coverage for 48 hours. A memorable moment.

title: Polls Show 8-Point Deficit — Crisis Meeting with Campaign
description: New polling shows you trailing by 8 points with 6 weeks to go. Your campaign manager says you need a major move. The safe path is incremental and probably loses. The bold path has risk but a real chance. This is the moment where your character as a leader gets tested most directly.

choices.text: Major policy announcement — something big, something real, something bold
choices.outcome: The announcement created genuine momentum. It was expensive and specific and voters believed it. The race tightened to 4 points. You were back in it.

choices.text: Voter mobilization — pour everything into turnout in the base
choices.outcome: The turnout operation was the most sophisticated in modern politics. Base enthusiasm was electric. Whether it was enough depended on how the math fell on election day.

choices.text: Pick a fight — attack the opponent's record hard
choices.outcome: The negative campaign tightened the race to 5 points. Your numbers didn't improve but theirs dropped. A race to the bottom that you entered from behind.

choices.text: Govern, don't campaign — make news with presidential action
choices.outcome: A major presidential action dominated the news cycle and reminded voters why they elected you. The dignity contrast with your opponent moved independent voters. The race tightened to 3 points.

title: Final Week — Everything on the Line
description: One week to election day. The polls are within the margin of error. Your campaign has resources for one more major push. Every decision this week will be analyzed for decades. This is what you've been working toward for four years.

choices.text: Barnstorm swing states — 14-state tour in 7 days
choices.outcome: The energy on the trail was electric. Crowds exceeded projections. The final rallies in Wisconsin and Michigan produced the intensity your campaign needed. You gave everything you had.

choices.text: Stay presidential — govern from the Oval Office, not the trail
choices.outcome: The statesmanlike approach drew a sharp contrast with your opponent's relentless negativity. Undecided voters rated the contrast favorably. You trusted your record and your people trusted you.

choices.text: Make a major surprise endorsement play — bring in the biggest surrogate you can
choices.outcome: The surrogate announcement dominated two news cycles. The enthusiasm boost was real and visible. The coalition it activated had been dormant. A smart tactical finishing move.

choices.text: Spend everything on digital — micro-targeted final push
choices.outcome: The data operation identified 2.3 million persuadable voters and hit them with precision messaging. The ground game confirmed the digital push was working. Modern campaigning at its best.

title: Major Hospital System Announces 40% Price Hike
description: The largest hospital network in America has announced average price increases of 40% citing staffing costs, drug price inflation, and insurance complexity. Medical bankruptcies hit 800,000 last year — the highest in recorded history. Every American has a healthcare horror story and they're all looking at you.

choices.text: Price transparency mandate and hospital billing reform
choices.outcome: The transparency requirements exposed egregious price disparities. Hospitals moderated increases under public pressure. Consumer savings were real if smaller than promised.

choices.text: All-payer rate setting — federal caps on what hospitals can charge
choices.outcome: The rate setting bill was the most aggressive healthcare regulation in American history. The hospital industry fought viciously. It passed narrowly. Access improved. Rural hospitals struggled.

choices.text: Medical debt cancellation — erase all existing medical bankruptcies
choices.outcome: 800,000 families got a fresh start. Consumer spending rebounded. Hospitals lobbied furiously against future debt cancellation. The immediate human impact was undeniable.

choices.text: Invest in community health centers and primary care capacity
choices.outcome: The investment reduced emergency room overuse and improved preventive care metrics. Long-term healthcare costs began bending. The impact was real and slow and rarely made headlines.

title: US Maternal Mortality Hits Developed World Record High
description: A new study confirms the US maternal mortality rate is now the highest in the developed world — and 3x higher for Black women. The data has sparked national outrage. OB-GYN deserts are growing. Healthcare providers are leaving the field citing legal uncertainty in states with new restrictions.

choices.text: Federal maternal health investment — fund OB-GYN training and rural access
choices.outcome: The investment added 12,000 OB-GYNs to underserved areas over three years. Maternal mortality began declining in year two. A genuine public health win.

choices.text: Executive order protecting healthcare providers from prosecution
choices.outcome: The order protected providers in legally uncertain states. Legal challenges flooded in immediately. The practical effect was limited but the signal to providers was meaningful.

choices.text: National maternal health emergency declaration and task force
choices.outcome: The emergency declaration unlocked federal resources and forced a national conversation. The task force's recommendations were implemented over 18 months. Progress was real.

choices.text: Frame it as a state issue — direct resources but decline federal role
choices.outcome: The states-rights framing was seen as abdication by medical professionals and women's health advocates. The crisis continued. The federal capacity to help went unused.

title: Youth Mental Health Emergency — Suicide Rates at Record High
description: The Surgeon General has issued an emergency advisory: youth suicide rates are at all-time highs, teen depression has doubled in a decade, and social media is cited as a contributing factor. Every parent in America is scared. School counselors are overwhelmed. The crisis is real and the political window to act is now.

choices.text: Mental health parity enforcement and school counselor funding surge
choices.outcome: The funding reached 45,000 schools within a year. Wait times for youth mental health services dropped 40%. A quiet, durable win that touched nearly every American family.

choices.text: Regulate social media algorithms for minors — age verification and limits
choices.outcome: The regulations were challenged immediately by tech companies. The European Union adopted similar rules the same week. A significant legislative achievement in a complex space.

choices.text: National suicide prevention infrastructure — 988 expansion and crisis centers
choices.outcome: The 988 crisis line went from underfunded to fully staffed. Response times dropped from 40 minutes to under 4. Measurable lives were saved. One of the less celebrated but most consequential decisions.

choices.text: Blame parents and screens — call for personal responsibility
choices.outcome: The personal responsibility framing was deeply unpopular with parents who were already overwhelmed and looking for help, not blame. The youth mental health crisis continued unaddressed.

title: Soviet Missiles Discovered 90 Miles From Florida
description: U-2 reconnaissance photos confirm it: a hostile nuclear power is installing medium-range ballistic missile sites in Cuba, capable of striking most of the continental United States within minutes of launch. Your Joint Chiefs are unanimous in recommending an immediate airstrike. Your UN Ambassador wants to expose the photos publicly first. Every option carries real risk of nuclear war. You have, by most estimates, about a week before the sites become operational.
historicalContext: Inspired by the Cuban Missile Crisis of October 1962, widely regarded as the closest the Cold War came to nuclear conflict. The actual resolution involved a naval quarantine, back-channel diplomacy, and a secret missile-removal agreement — not necessarily the path your choices here will take.

choices.text: Order an immediate airstrike on the missile sites before they're operational
choices.outcome: The strike destroyed most sites but Soviet casualties triggered an immediate military response elsewhere. The world held its breath for nine days. History will remember whether restraint or force kept the peace — this time, you chose force.

choices.text: Impose a naval quarantine and demand removal through back-channel diplomacy
choices.outcome: Thirteen days of the tensest diplomacy in the nuclear age. A secret deal — their missiles out of Cuba, yours quietly out of Turkey — defused the crisis without a shot fired. Historians will study this for generations.

choices.text: Go public immediately with the UN photos to mobilize international pressure
choices.outcome: The public exposure cornered the adversary diplomatically but removed their ability to back down quietly. The standoff lasted longer and the off-ramps narrowed. Tense, but it held.

choices.text: Open direct secret negotiations before taking any public action
choices.outcome: The quiet approach bought time but drew criticism once it became public that you'd known for days without acting visibly. The missiles came out. The political cost of appearing passive lingered.

title: Major Oil Producers Announce Embargo Over Foreign Policy
description: In retaliation for US support of an ally during a regional war, the largest oil-producing nations have announced an embargo on exports to the United States. Gas lines are already forming. Prices are projected to quadruple within months. Your Energy Secretary wants emergency rationing. Your Treasury Secretary wants to ride it out and let markets adjust. Winter is approaching.
historicalContext: Inspired by the 1973 OPEC oil embargo, triggered by Western support for Israel during the Yom Kippur War. The embargo quadrupled oil prices and contributed to a recession across the industrialized world.

choices.text: Implement gas rationing and a national speed limit to conserve fuel
choices.outcome: Rationing was unpopular but effective. Gas lines shortened within weeks. Drivers furious about a 55mph speed limit became a defining image of the crisis. Conservation became a national habit that outlasted the embargo.

choices.text: Let markets adjust naturally — no rationing, no price controls
choices.outcome: Prices spiked uncontrolled. Gas lines stretched for blocks. The free-market approach was economically defensible and politically brutal. The embargo's economic damage was deeper and longer than rationing would have produced.

choices.text: Launch an emergency national energy independence initiative
choices.outcome: The crisis became the catalyst for a generational investment in domestic energy production and efficiency standards. The pain was real in year one. The strategic vulnerability it fixed mattered for decades.

choices.text: Negotiate directly with producing nations to lift the embargo
choices.outcome: Diplomacy eventually worked, five months later, after the underlying regional conflict cooled. Critics said you should have acted on energy independence instead of waiting on others. The embargo lifted. The vulnerability remained.

title: Bank Runs Spread Across the Country — Markets in Free Fall
description: What began as one regional bank's collapse has metastasized into a full-blown banking panic. Depositors are pulling cash from institutions nationwide. The stock market has lost a third of its value in weeks. Breadlines are forming in major cities. Your economic advisors are split between dramatic federal intervention and waiting for the market to find its own bottom.
historicalContext: Inspired by the banking crisis of the early Great Depression (1930-1933), when thousands of US banks failed and a new administration declared a nationwide bank holiday in its first days in office to stop the panic.

choices.text: Declare a national bank holiday — close all banks for inspection and reopen only solvent ones
choices.outcome: The bold, decisive action stopped the panic almost overnight. When banks reopened days later under federal certification, deposits flowed back in rather than out. A textbook case of decisive crisis leadership.

choices.text: Let weak banks fail — protect only systemically critical institutions
choices.outcome: Thousands of smaller banks failed. Rural communities lost their only financial institutions entirely. The strategy was fiscally conservative and devastated ordinary depositors who lost their life savings.

choices.text: Establish federal deposit insurance to restore public confidence
choices.outcome: The insurance program took months to fully implement but fundamentally changed Americans' relationship with banks. Bank runs became rare for the rest of the century. A structural fix, not just a crisis response.

choices.text: Address the nation directly and calmly to restore confidence without drastic action
choices.outcome: The address was reassuring in tone but light on substance. Confidence improved modestly. Without structural action, smaller panics continued to flare up over the following months.

title: Americans Taken Hostage After Embassy Stormed
description: Revolutionary forces have stormed the US embassy in a country undergoing violent upheaval and are holding dozens of American diplomatic personnel hostage. Demands include the return of the former allied head of state, who fled to the US for medical treatment. Negotiations have stalled for months. Your Joint Chiefs have a rescue plan. Your diplomats warn it's a long shot with catastrophic downside if it fails.
historicalContext: Inspired by the Iran hostage crisis of 1979-1981, in which 52 Americans were held for 444 days. A military rescue attempt (Operation Eagle Claw) failed due to equipment problems in the Iranian desert, and the hostages were ultimately released through diplomatic negotiation, on the day of the next inauguration.

choices.text: Authorize the military rescue operation
choices.outcome: The operation failed before reaching the hostages — equipment failures in harsh terrain forced an abort, with casualties among the rescue team. The failure became a symbol of American vulnerability that haunted the rest of the term.

choices.text: Maintain economic sanctions and continue diplomatic negotiations exclusively
choices.outcome: Patient diplomacy was politically costly in the short term — every week of captivity dominated the news and eroded approval — but ultimately secured release through negotiation rather than force, with no additional lives lost.

choices.text: Freeze all of the hostile nation's assets and escalate economic pressure
choices.outcome: The frozen assets gave significant leverage for the eventual negotiated release, though it took many more months. Domestically, the prolonged crisis remained a constant drumbeat of bad news regardless of the strategy.

choices.text: Offer to return the former head of state in exchange for the hostages
choices.outcome: The offer was rejected by the revolutionary government as insufficient, and was widely condemned domestically as appeasement that betrayed an ally who had trusted American protection. A costly move that achieved nothing.

title: Steelworkers Strike Threatens to Cripple Wartime Production
description: Hundreds of thousands of steelworkers have walked off the job over wages and working conditions, just as the nation's defense industry depends on steady steel production. Industry executives want you to break the strike by seizing the mills under emergency powers. Labor unions, a core part of your coalition, are furious at the idea. The Supreme Court has not yet weighed in on whether you even have that authority.
historicalContext: Inspired by the 1952 steel strike and the Youngstown Sheet & Tube Co. v. Sawyer Supreme Court case, in which the Court ruled that a President lacked the constitutional authority to seize private steel mills to prevent a labor strike, even during wartime — a landmark check on executive power.

choices.text: Seize the steel mills under emergency executive authority
choices.outcome: The seizure kept production running, but legal challenges moved immediately. Months later, the Supreme Court ruled the seizure unconstitutional — a defining precedent limiting executive power that would be cited for the rest of American history.

choices.text: Stay out of it — let the strike and negotiations run their course
choices.outcome: The strike lasted weeks longer than industry wanted, denting defense production schedules. Labor felt respected. Industry was furious. The eventual settlement came through negotiation, not force.

choices.text: Personally mediate between labor and management to broker a settlement
choices.outcome: Marathon negotiating sessions in the Oval Office produced a settlement in days rather than months. Both sides claimed partial victory. The hands-on approach was widely praised as genuine leadership.

choices.text: Invoke emergency labor law to force an 80-day cooling-off period
choices.outcome: The cooling-off period bought time without the constitutional risk of seizure. Labor saw it as a delay tactic favoring management. Production resumed. The underlying dispute simmered, unresolved, beneath the surface.

title: Novel Respiratory Virus Detected — First Cases on US Soil
description: Health officials confirm the first domestic cases of a novel respiratory virus that has been spreading internationally for weeks. Early data is incomplete and contradictory — some models suggest a mild flu-like illness, others suggest something far deadlier. The CDC wants to move cautiously and avoid panic. Your political team is split on whether early, visible action helps or just spooks markets before there's enough data to justify it.
historicalContext: Inspired by the early weeks of the COVID-19 pandemic in early 2020, when initial uncertainty about transmissibility and severity created genuine disagreement among public health officials and governments worldwide about the right early response.

choices.text: Move aggressively early — travel restrictions and public health emergency, even with incomplete data
choices.outcome: The early action was criticized as alarmist at the time — markets dropped, businesses complained. Weeks later, as case counts confirmed the threat was real, the early measures were credited with meaningfully reducing the eventual death toll.

choices.text: Wait for more conclusive data before taking visible action
choices.outcome: The wait-and-see approach avoided early economic disruption but cost critical weeks. By the time the threat was undeniable, containment was no longer realistic — only mitigation. The early calm became a political liability in hindsight.

choices.text: Quietly surge funding to vaccine and treatment research without public alarm
choices.outcome: The research investment paid off later — when therapeutics and vaccines arrived faster than historical precedent suggested possible. In the immediate term, critics said the response looked passive on the public health side.

choices.text: Convene international health authorities for a coordinated global response
choices.outcome: International coordination improved data-sharing and slowed cross-border spread modestly. Domestically, the multilateral framing read as less decisive than voters wanted from their own President in a moment of fear.

title: Budget Standoff Triggers Longest Government Shutdown in History
description: Congress and the White House are deadlocked over a single contentious spending provision. The shutdown has now stretched past a month — the longest in the nation's history. 800,000 federal workers are furloughed or working without pay. Air traffic controllers are calling in sick at unsustainable rates. Neither side believes blinking first is politically survivable.
historicalContext: Inspired by the 35-day government shutdown of December 2018–January 2019, the longest in US history, which ended after air traffic disruptions at major airports forced a resolution without the disputed funding being secured.

choices.text: Hold firm on the disputed provision — no compromise until Congress funds it
choices.outcome: The standoff dragged on for weeks longer. Air travel disruptions from unpaid controllers eventually forced an end to the shutdown — without the disputed funding. Holding firm cost more than it gained, but the base respected the fight.

choices.text: Reopen the government immediately and continue fighting for the provision separately
choices.outcome: Federal workers got back pay quickly and public relief was immediate. The disputed provision never got funded on its own. Your most committed supporters called it capitulation. Most of the country called it relief.

choices.text: Negotiate a partial compromise — some funding, some concessions on both sides
choices.outcome: Neither side got what they wanted, which by the strange math of legislative compromise meant it was probably the right outcome. The shutdown ended in days rather than weeks. Both bases grumbled. The government reopened.

choices.text: Declare a national emergency to fund the provision through executive authority
choices.outcome: The emergency declaration ended the shutdown but triggered immediate, lasting legal challenges over the scope of executive emergency powers — a fight that outlasted the shutdown itself by years.

title: Rival Power Launches First Satellite — National Confidence Shaken
description: A geopolitical rival has successfully launched the world's first artificial satellite, beating the United States into space. The technological shock has triggered genuine public fear that the same rocket technology could deliver a nuclear warhead to American soil. Congress wants immediate, dramatic investment in science and defense. Your budget team warns the panic may be larger than the actual strategic shift.
historicalContext: Inspired by the launch of Sputnik in October 1957, which triggered the "Sputnik crisis" — a wave of American anxiety about technological and military inferiority that led directly to the creation of NASA and a massive increase in federal science education funding.

choices.text: Launch a crash national science and space program to close the gap fast
choices.outcome: The crash program restored public confidence within months and laid the groundwork for dominance in space exploration within a decade. Expensive, but it transformed American science education and federal research investment for a generation.

choices.text: Emphasize calm — the strategic threat is overstated, no crash program needed
choices.outcome: The reassurance was technically accurate but politically tone-deaf. Public anxiety didn't subside on the strength of an argument; it subsided when visible action followed months later, after the political damage was done.

choices.text: Quietly accelerate military rocket programs without a public science initiative
choices.outcome: The military-only approach closed the strategic gap effectively but missed the public confidence dimension entirely. Voters wanted to see something solving the problem, not just trust that something was happening behind closed doors.

choices.text: Create a new civilian space agency separate from military programs
choices.outcome: Separating civilian space exploration from military rocketry sent a deliberate signal about peaceful intent while still building the underlying technical capability. The new agency became a source of national pride for generations.

title: Inflation and Unemployment Rise Together — Economists Have No Playbook
description: Conventional economics says inflation and unemployment shouldn't rise simultaneously, but that's exactly what's happening. Prices are climbing while businesses lay off workers. Standard tools — raise rates to fight inflation, or stimulate spending to fight unemployment — each risk making the other problem worse. Your economic team is openly divided on which threat to prioritize.
historicalContext: Inspired by the stagflation crisis of the 1970s, when high inflation and high unemployment occurred simultaneously — a combination that conventional Keynesian economics of the era struggled to explain or address, ultimately reshaping macroeconomic policy for decades.

choices.text: Prioritize fighting inflation with aggressive monetary tightening, accept higher unemployment short-term
choices.outcome: Unemployment climbed sharply and a recession followed within the year. Inflation did eventually break. The political pain of the recession landed entirely on your watch; the inflation relief showed up after, credited to whoever came next.

choices.text: Prioritize jobs with stimulus spending, accept inflation will run hotter
choices.outcome: Unemployment improved and the immediate political reaction was positive. Inflation accelerated further, compounding the underlying problem and setting up a more painful reckoning down the road.

choices.text: Impose temporary wage and price controls to break the inflationary spiral directly
choices.outcome: Controls slowed inflation on paper while creating shortages and black markets underneath. When controls were lifted, prices snapped back up even higher in a painful correction. A short-term fix with a real long-term cost.

choices.text: Pursue a narrow, targeted approach — sector-specific relief rather than economy-wide moves
choices.outcome: The targeted approach avoided the worst downside of either extreme but also avoided decisively fixing either problem. Both inflation and unemployment lingered, neither catastrophic, neither resolved, through the rest of the term.

title: Secret White House Recordings Reportedly Exist
description: A former staffer has gone public claiming the White House has been secretly recording Oval Office conversations — including conversations relevant to an ongoing investigation into senior officials. Congress is demanding the tapes. Your legal team says you may have a claim of executive privilege, but it has never been tested this directly before. Refusing will look like guilt. Releasing them sets a precedent no future President may want.
historicalContext: Inspired by the Watergate tapes controversy of 1973-1974, when the existence of a secret White House taping system became central to a constitutional crisis over executive privilege, ultimately decided by the Supreme Court in United States v. Nixon.

choices.text: Release the tapes in full — let the public and Congress hear everything
choices.outcome: Full release ended the immediate cover-up narrative but exposed conversations that were damaging on their own terms. Painful, but the story moved from 'what are they hiding' to a more survivable 'here's what was said.'

choices.text: Claim executive privilege and refuse to release them
choices.outcome: The refusal triggered an immediate legal battle and a constitutional showdown over the limits of executive privilege. The fight itself became the story, and the perception of obstruction did more damage than disclosure likely would have.

choices.text: Release heavily redacted transcripts instead of the actual recordings
choices.outcome: The redactions were immediately read as evidence something worse was being hidden. Congress and the press treated the partial release as confirmation of a cover-up rather than the good-faith compromise it was intended to be.

choices.text: Propose an independent special counsel to review the tapes privately first
choices.outcome: The independent review bought time and added a credible neutral voice to the process. It also meant living with the uncertainty for months longer, with the story never fully leaving the headlines while the review proceeded.

title: Your Reelection Campaign Has Officially Launched
description: Your campaign team says it's time. The opposition has been running ads in swing states for months. Donors want a strategy, the base wants a message, and the press wants access. How you enter this race will define it.

choices.text: Run on your record — major rally, highlight accomplishments
choices.outcome: The base turns out in force. The rally dominates the news cycle. But the opposition immediately starts picking apart your record.

choices.text: Go negative early — define the opponent before they define themselves
choices.outcome: Your opponent's negatives rise, but so does the temperature. Allies abroad express concern about the tone of American democracy.

choices.text: Announce a bold new policy agenda — give people something to vote for
choices.outcome: The new platform energizes the base. The price tag raises eyebrows, but voters respond to a vision.

choices.text: Stay presidential — govern, don't campaign
choices.outcome: Allies respect the restraint. Your campaign team is furious. You're watching ground slip in swing states.

title: Congressional Opponents Launch Investigation Into Your Administration
description: The opposition has subpoenaed senior White House staff and is demanding documents going back three years. It may be a fishing expedition — or they may have something. Either way, it will dominate the news for months.

choices.text: Full cooperation — release everything, nothing to hide
choices.outcome: The transparency play works with independents. Your own party is furious you didn't fight it. The documents take weeks to review.

choices.text: Executive privilege — fight every subpoena in court
choices.outcome: The base loves the fight. The legal battle drags on for months. The press covers it daily.

choices.text: Negotiate — cooperate selectively on the least damaging documents
choices.outcome: A messy middle ground. No one is happy. But the investigation moves slower than it would have.

choices.text: Counter-attack — demand investigation of the investigators
choices.outcome: The base erupts in support. The country is more divided than ever. International observers are troubled.

title: Senior Advisor Leaks Damaging Internal Memos to the Press
description: A major newspaper is running a story based on internal White House communications. Someone on the inside is talking. The memos describe private disagreements that will embarrass the administration. You have 24 hours before publication.

choices.text: Get ahead of it — hold a press conference and contextualize the memos
choices.outcome: The transparency gambit works better than expected. The story still runs, but you've already shaped the frame.

choices.text: Find and fire the leaker before the story runs
choices.outcome: You find someone to blame and make an example of them. It sends a message — but the story runs anyway, now with a sidebar about the firing.

choices.text: Legal threat — warn the newspaper about national security implications
choices.outcome: Press freedom groups mobilize immediately. The story runs anyway, now with a lede about administration press intimidation.

choices.text: No comment — let it pass
choices.outcome: The silence reads as guilt. The story dominates for a week with no counter-narrative.

title: Midterm Elections: The Country Votes on Your First Two Years
description: Midterms are always a referendum on the sitting president. The question isn't whether you'll lose seats — history says you will — it's how many. The result will define whether your second two years are productive or paralyzed.

choices.text: Heavy campaign investment — travel, rally, fundraise to protect the majority
choices.outcome: The investment pays off. You hold more seats than the historical average. The majority is thin but workable.

choices.text: Focus on governing — let the record speak for itself
choices.outcome: The record isn't enough. You lose seats. The majority is gone or diminished. Governing just got harder.

choices.text: Push a major last-minute policy announcement to energize turnout
choices.outcome: The October surprise works. Turnout among your base is strong. The opposition calls it a stunt.

choices.text: Distance yourself from vulnerable incumbents — protect the brand
choices.outcome: Party unity is shattered. Several members who lost feel abandoned. You'll pay for this in caucus for years.

title: Foreign Adversary Tests Your Resolve in Final Year of Term
description: Intelligence suggests a major adversary is probing for weakness in your final year, calculating that a lame-duck administration won't respond forcefully. Allies are watching to see if the US can be pushed around in an election year.

choices.text: Respond with overwhelming force — make clear lame-duck doesn't mean weak
choices.outcome: The response is decisive. Allies are reassured. The adversary backs down. The political win helps in the polls.

choices.text: Diplomatic back-channel — resolve it quietly without escalation
choices.outcome: You prevent escalation. Nobody outside intelligence circles knows how close it got. Critics say you were weak.

choices.text: Warn publicly and draw a red line
choices.outcome: The public statement firms up the position. Markets wobble briefly. The adversary pauses — for now.

choices.text: Defer to your successor — this decision belongs to the next administration
choices.outcome: The adversary takes it as confirmation of weakness. Allies are stunned. It will be the defining image of the end of your term.

title: Major Party Fundraising Gala
description: With re-election on the horizon, your campaign has organized a high-dollar fundraising gala. Donors expect face time. Your schedule is already packed with governing. Skipping it risks the campaign's war chest; attending risks the optics of a president distracted by politics while problems remain unsolved.

choices.text: Attend personally and work the room all night
choices.outcome: The war chest swells. Photos of you laughing with donors make the morning shows, and not in a flattering light.

choices.text: Send the Vice President in your place
choices.outcome: Donors are mildly disappointed but understanding. The optics stay clean. Fundraising totals come in lower than hoped.

choices.text: Skip it entirely and hold a working dinner on policy instead
choices.outcome: The press praises your focus. Campaign finance staff are furious — the fundraising gap with your opponent widens.

choices.text: Attend briefly, deliver remarks, then leave early
choices.outcome: A measured showing. Nobody's fully satisfied, but nobody's especially upset either.

title: The First Presidential Debate
description: Ninety minutes, one stage, no script. Your opponent has been rehearsing attack lines for weeks. Your debate prep team is split on strategy — some want you aggressive, some want you presidential and above the fray. The performance will dominate news coverage for the following week regardless of what you actually say.

choices.text: Go on the offensive and attack your opponent's record directly
choices.outcome: Your base loves it. Pundits call it a mixed bag — sharp, but undignified. Clips of the sharpest exchange go viral within hours.

choices.text: Stay above the fray and focus on substantive policy
choices.outcome: Fact-checkers rate you highly. It doesn't make for great television, and your opponent's attack lines land largely unanswered.

choices.text: Lean on humor to defuse the sharpest attacks
choices.outcome: A couple of lines land well and dominate the highlight reels. Some voters find it too glib for the moment.

choices.text: Play it safe and avoid any real news
choices.outcome: The safest possible ninety minutes. Commentators call it forgettable, which your opponent's campaign happily amplifies.

title: Opponent Launches Brutal Attack Ad Campaign
description: A wave of attack ads is airing in every swing state, built around your administration's most controversial moments — some fairly characterized, others badly distorted. Your campaign manager wants a response strategy locked in before the news cycle moves on and the ads' framing hardens into conventional wisdom.

choices.text: Launch an equally aggressive counter-ad campaign
choices.outcome: The airwaves turn into a slugfest. Your base is energized. Independents tell pollsters they're tired of both sides.

choices.text: Publicly fact-check the ads point by point
choices.outcome: The fact-check gets real pickup in the press. It also keeps the ads' claims in the news for another cycle.

choices.text: Threaten legal action against the most misleading claims
choices.outcome: Free speech advocates in both parties criticize the move. The ads keep airing, now with extra attention.

choices.text: Ignore it and stay focused on the day-to-day business of governing
choices.outcome: You look presidential by comparison. Campaign staff worry the unanswered attacks are sinking in with undecided voters.

title: Swing State Campaign Stop
description: Your campaign has scheduled a rally in a state that could decide the election. Local organizers, national strategists, and your own instincts don't fully agree on what the crowd needs to hear tonight.

choices.text: Fire up the base with red-meat rhetoric
choices.outcome: The crowd roars. Clips travel fast on social media — thrilling to supporters, alarming to everyone else watching.

choices.text: Pitch a moderate, unifying message to undecided voters
choices.outcome: The speech plays well with commentators calling for less division. Some of your most committed supporters call it flat.

choices.text: Focus entirely on local jobs and issues specific to this state
choices.outcome: Local coverage is glowing. National media barely covers it — it wasn't built for that audience anyway.

choices.text: Cancel the rally and send senior surrogates instead
choices.outcome: Local organizers are frustrated. The decision barely registers nationally, but it costs you momentum on the ground.

title: Party Leaders Debate Endorsing You for a Second Term
description: Behind closed doors, senior members of your own party are debating how enthusiastically to back your re-election. Some want to campaign hard on your record. Others are quietly keeping their distance, hedging in case your numbers slide further before Election Day.

choices.text: Call in every favor to lock down a unified, full-throated endorsement
choices.outcome: The party closes ranks publicly. Privately, some members remind you they expect this debt repaid after the election.

choices.text: Let each member endorse on their own terms and timeline
choices.outcome: A patchwork of endorsements trickles out over several weeks. It reads as authentic, if a little disorganized.

choices.text: Lean into running as an outsider, independent of party machinery
choices.outcome: It plays well with voters tired of politics as usual. Party leadership is furious at being publicly sidelined.

choices.text: Focus on courting a handful of high-profile crossover endorsements instead
choices.outcome: The crossover endorsements generate real buzz. Your own party's rank-and-file feel taken for granted.
